# AGENTS_MD_IS_ORIENTATION_SURFACE

**Tier:** Shipped default — generated by `dome init` for every new vault; refreshed by the templated-section merge (today `dome init` re-runs; v1.x: the reserved `dome doctor --repair` verb plus the `dome.health.agents-md-template-drift` garden-phase processor). Vault owners can edit the user-prose section freely; the templated sections regenerate on demand.

**Statement:** The vault root carries an `AGENTS.md` file that is the canonical orientation surface for agentic harnesses interacting with the vault. The file teaches the agent the vault's conventions, declared page types, named command-triggered view processors (the `dome <command>` surface), the active invariant set, extension-bundle conventions, and any vault-owner-authored prose (delimited via HTML comments). A Claude-Code-compatible `CLAUDE.md` shim at vault root points at `AGENTS.md` so Claude Code's auto-loading mechanism picks up the orientation transparently.

`AGENTS.md`'s contents are split into **templated sections** (regenerated by the merge mechanism — see §Tier above — from the vault's current `.dome/config.yaml`, enabled invariant set, declared page types, loaded `dome.*` first-party + third-party extension bundles, the command-triggered view processors each bundle contributes, and loaded extension-bundle preamble fragments) and a **user-prose section** (delimited by `<!-- BEGIN user-prose --> ... <!-- END user-prose -->` comments) that the vault owner edits freely. The user-prose section is preserved verbatim across all merge runs.

**Extension-bundle preamble fragments.** Each loaded extension bundle under `<vault>/.dome/extensions/<name>/` may contribute a `preamble.md` fragment that becomes a subsection of AGENTS.md's templated `## Extension conventions` block. Fragments render in bundle load order (first-party `dome.*` bundles alphabetical, then third-party alphabetical). The fragments teach the agent the bundle's conventions — for example, the first-party `dome.daily` bundle's preamble explains the daily/weekly cadence, the Obsidian-Tasks-plugin syntax convention, and the carry-forward semantics so an agent writing into the vault knows how to maintain dailies correctly. The fragments are part of the *templated* surface, not user prose — they regenerate on every merge run and are tied to which bundles are currently loaded.

**Why:** Per [[VISION]] §"Two surface patterns" and [[wiki/specs/harnesses]] §"The compiler-boundary contract", `AGENTS.md` is one of the four load-bearing surfaces of the compiler boundary — the orientation surface for agentic harnesses. Without `AGENTS.md` (or with a stale one), an agent arriving at a Dome vault would have no way to know its conventions, named command surface, or active invariants short of reading the SDK source — and most agents wouldn't. `AGENTS.md` is what makes "the agent arrives oriented" work without each harness implementing per-vault discovery logic.

Why this is shipped-default rather than opt-in: the cost of an absent `AGENTS.md` (silent agent confusion: unknown page types, unknown command-triggered processors, unknown active invariants, unknown frontmatter requirements) is high and silent; the cost of a present `AGENTS.md` is a few KB of markdown at vault root. The asymmetry is decisive.

**Structural enforcement:**

- `dome init` writes `AGENTS.md` (templated sections + an empty user-prose section with the delimiters in place) and `CLAUDE.md` (the shim, content `See AGENTS.md.`) at vault root. The initial git commit captures both, then the engine runs the adoption loop and adopts. When the new vault has no extension bundles loaded beyond the first-party `dome.*` set (the shipped-default state), the `## Extension conventions` templated section renders the `dome.*` preamble fragments and nothing else (no empty header).
- The merge mechanism regenerates the templated sections of `AGENTS.md` from the current vault state (config, enabled invariants, declared page types, loaded extension bundles + their command-triggered processors + preamble fragments). The user-prose section is parsed via the delimiter comments and re-emitted verbatim. If the file is missing entirely, the merge recreates it (treating the user-prose section as empty). Adding an extension bundle to `<vault>/.dome/extensions/` and running the merge adds its preamble fragment to the templated section and adds its command-triggered processors to the named-commands subsection; removing the bundle and running the merge removes both. The merge runs today on `dome init` re-runs; v1.x adds the reserved `dome doctor --repair` verb and the `dome.health.agents-md-template-drift` garden-phase processor as additional triggers.
- Drift is reported as DiagnosticEffects emitted by the `dome.lint.lint-report` view-phase processor (per [[wiki/specs/processors]] §"First-party processors") and surfaced via `dome show diagnostics`: `AGENTS.md` missing → diagnostic; templated sections out of sync with current config → diagnostic; `CLAUDE.md` shim missing or pointing at the wrong file → diagnostic; loaded extension bundles whose preamble fragments are absent from the `## Extension conventions` section → diagnostic (the fragment was contributed but the merge hasn't run since). The v1.x `dome doctor` verb runs the same lint as part of its check set.

**Counter-example (what this prevents):** A user runs `dome init` on a new vault. Six months later, they install the third-party `acme.calendar-sync` bundle which contributes a `dome calendar` command-triggered view processor. Without the merge mechanism, the `AGENTS.md` orientation would still describe only the original command surface. The agent reads the stale orientation, doesn't know `dome calendar` exists, and falls back to inferior workflows. With the merge running (today on `dome init` re-runs; v1.x via `dome doctor --repair` and the planned template-drift garden processor), the templated sections regenerate from the loaded bundle set, the agent learns `dome calendar` is available, and uses it.

**Test guarantee:** A regression test scaffolds a fresh vault with `dome init`, asserts `AGENTS.md` exists with the expected templated sections (per [[wiki/matrices/built-in-extensions-x-phase]]) and an empty user-prose delimiter pair. A second test adds user-prose content, mutates `.dome/config.yaml` (enabling a new invariant), invokes `mergeAgentsMd` directly via the SDK (the merge mechanism — surfaced via `dome init` re-runs today; via `dome doctor --repair` in v1.x), asserts the templated sections updated and the user-prose section is preserved byte-for-byte. A third test deletes `AGENTS.md` entirely, runs `dome lint`, asserts `dome.lint.lint-report` emits a diagnostic naming the missing file (visible via `dome show diagnostics`). A fourth test installs the hello-world fixture bundle into the test vault's `.dome/extensions/`, invokes the merge, asserts the `## Extension conventions` section contains the bundle's preamble fragment as a subsection, removes the bundle, re-invokes the merge, asserts the fragment is removed AND the user-prose section is still preserved byte-for-byte.

**Operational notes:**

- `AGENTS.md` is per-vault, not per-codebase. Two vaults on the same machine have independent `AGENTS.md` files reflecting their independent configurations and bundle sets.
- The file is vault-owned (committed to git, edited by the user); the SDK never clobbers the user-prose section. The templated sections are explicitly fenced off via the delimiter comments so the user knows what is and isn't safe to edit by hand.
- Extension-bundle preamble fragments are templated content, not user prose — editing them in AGENTS.md directly is a soft anti-pattern (the next merge regenerates them from the bundle's `preamble.md`). Bundle authors edit the fragment at its source; users who want to override a bundle's preamble fork the bundle.
- The MCP server's `instructions` payload (per [[wiki/specs/mcp-surface]]) mirrors AGENTS.md as the secondary delivery channel for harnesses that mount MCP, regenerated alongside it — extension-bundle preamble fragments propagate into `instructions` automatically. The mirror is structural (both surfaces are renderings of the same `AbstractSurface.instructions` string built by `buildAbstractSurface(vault)`), not best-effort.
- The `CLAUDE.md` shim exists because Claude Code's auto-load convention currently prefers `CLAUDE.md`; removable once `AGENTS.md` auto-load is universal across harnesses.
